description_short_en: A global icon of Swedish vodka, Absolut is renowned for its exceptional purity and continuous winter wheat distillation.
description_full_en: Recognized worldwide as a pioneer of premium vodka, Absolut commands a prominent place in modern cocktail culture. Produced exclusively in Åhus, Sweden, the spirit is crafted from locally grown winter wheat and pristine deep-well water. Their signature continuous distillation process ensures a clean, exceptionally smooth profile without any added sugar. On the palate, Absolut delivers a distinctively pure, crisp character with a subtle touch of dried fruit and a smooth, mellow finish.

Their philosophy champions the unique microclimates of each parcel, ensuring authentic expression of Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. description_short_en: The benchmark producer of Valpolicella, Allegrini is globally celebrated for its masterful Amarone and innovative single-vineyard wines.
description_full_en: Widely regarded as the leading light of the Valpolicella region, Allegrini commands immense respect among Italian wine collectors worldwide. For centuries, the family has championed the Corvina grape, perfecting the traditional 'appassimento' method of drying grapes to achieve extraordinary concentration. They revolutionized the region by introducing single-vineyard expressions like La Poja and Palazzo della Torre. In the glass, Allegrini wines deliver a heavy-hitting, luxurious profile of rich dark cherries, velvety dark chocolate, and warm, lingering baking spices.

classification_scope: Red Wine, White Wine, and Rose Wine
description_short_en: One of South Australia's oldest family-owned wineries, Angove is celebrated for its premium McLaren Vale expressions and pioneering organic viticulture.
description_full_en: Recognized as a foundational pillar of South Australian winemaking, Angove Family Winemakers boasts a rich heritage dating back to 1886. Today, they lead the industry as the country's largest certified organic grape grower and winemaker, with a strong focus on their premium McLaren Vale vineyards. Their sustainable philosophy ensures that their Shiraz, Cabernet, and Grenache authentically reflect the coastal Mediterranean climate. On the palate, Angove’s premium reds deliver a signature, vibrant profile of ripe dark berries, subtle earthiness, and elegantly structured tannins.

classification_scope: Red Wine, White Wine, and Sparkling Wine
description_short_en: A legendary force in Italian winemaking, Antinori is celebrated for shaping Chianti Classico and pioneering the Super Tuscan movement.
description_full_en: Considered royalty in the world of Italian wine, the Antinori family commands an unrivaled legacy spanning over six centuries and 26 generations. They famously revolutionized Tuscan viticulture by creating iconic Super Tuscans like Tignanello and Solaia, blending native Sangiovese with international varieties. Their vast portfolio encompasses prestigious estates across Chianti Classico, Bolgheri, and beyond. On the palate, Antinori's flagship reds deliver a masterful, heavy-hitting profile of ripe black fruits, crushed violets, savory herbs, and deeply integrated, velvety tannins.

parent_region: Montagne de Reims, Champagne AOC, France — production centred in Rilly-la-Montagne and Chigny-les-Roses
description_short_en: Armand de Brignac broke the conventional NV Champagne model by insisting on a strict trio-of-vintages assemblage (rather than a dominant base year topped up with reserves), meaning each release is a deliberate equal-weight blend of three…
description_full_en: Founded 2006 (brand launch) by Jean-Jacques Cattier; underlying Cattier family winemaking estate established 1763 in Chigny-les-Roses. Owner: Joint venture: Shawn 'Jay-Z' Carter (50%) and Moët Hennessy / LVMH (50%, acquired February 2021). Region: Montagne de Reims, Champagne AOC, France — production centred in Rilly-la-Montagne and Chigny-les-Roses. Classification: Champagne AOC — Prestige Cuvée (tête de cuvée); non-vintage multi-assemblage; no official 1855-style classification applies to Champagne houses. Style: Armand de Brignac is distinguished by its strict three-vintage assemblage philosophy — every cuvée blends exactly three distinct harvest years rather than the conventional base-vintage-plus-reserve-wine approach, delivering layered complexity with consistent house character. The dosage liqueur itself is aged one year in new French oak barrels before addition, imparting a signature toasted brioche, vanilla, and pastry complexity that sets it apart from peers such as Dom Pérignon (single vintage) or Krug Grande Cuvée (multi-vintage but broader reserve pool). Production is deliberately tiny — roughly 60,000 bottles annually — with each bottle hand-finished in metallised coating and hand-applied French pewter labels, making it simultaneously one of the most labour-intensive and visually distinctive prestige cuvées in Champagne. Site: Cattier family owns 33 hectares of Premier Cru vineyards across Rilly-la-Montagne, Chigny-les-Roses, and Ludes on the Montagne de Reims — chalky, cool-climate hillside terroir particularly suited to Pinot Noir. Chigny-les-Roses holds Premier Cru status. Additional fruit is sourced from trusted growers in Premier and Grand Cru villages including Verzenay (Pinot Noir), Avize, Cramant, and Oger (Chardonnay) on the Côte des Blancs. Vineyards are HVE Level 3 certified for high environmental value. The Cattier cellars at Rilly-la-Montagne descend over 30 metres (119+ steps) underground — among the deepest in Champagne — with Gothic, Renaissance, and Roman architectural levels; Armand de Brignac is aged in a dedicated gated section of the deepest level. Vinification: Hand-harvested grapes pressed in one of two family-owned wineries using traditional vertical Coquard presses (not pneumatic membrane presses), allowing slow, gentle extraction. Only the very first and freshest portion of the tête de cuvée press (from 4,000 kg lots yielding 20.5 hL allowable) is selected. Base wines vinified separately; sources indicate concrete tanks are used to develop creamy texture while preserving terroir character. Three distinct harvest years are then assembled into each non-vintage cuvée. Aging: Minimum 3 years on lees in chalk cellars for Brut Gold (some sources cite 4–5 years for certain assemblages; prestige cuvées may extend to 5+ years). Traditional hand riddling (remuage) over approximately one month. Dosage liqueur aged one year in new French oak barrels before addition at disgorgement. Bottling: Non-vintage; dosed (not zero-dosage). Brut Gold dosage: 9 g/L. Brut Rosé dosage: 8.5 g/L. Demi-Sec: 34 g/L. Bottled by hand using a traditional device; each bottle hand-metallised and hand-labelled with French pewter ace-of-spades motif. No fining/filtration status not publicly specified. ABV not stated on official materials (standard Champagne ~12–12.5%). Blend/recipe: Brut Gold (flagship): 40% Pinot Noir, 40% Chardonnay, 20% Pinot Meunier — blend of three vintages from Montagne de Reims, Vallée de la Marne, and Côte des Blancs. Brut Rosé: 50–55% Pinot Noir, 25–40% Pinot Meunier, 10–25% Chardonnay (sources vary slightly), with 12–18% still red Pinot Noir wine from old vines in Chigny-les-Roses added for colour and intensity. Blanc de Blancs: 100% Chardonnay from Côte des Blancs and Montagne de Reims. Blanc de Noirs: 100% Pinot Noir from Premier and Grand Cru villages including Verzenay and Chigny-les-Roses (fewer than 3,000 bottles per assemblage). Demi-Sec: 40% Pinot Noir, 40% Chardonnay, 20% Pinot Meunier. Must know: Armand de Brignac broke the conventional NV Champagne model by insisting on a strict trio-of-vintages assemblage (rather than a dominant base year topped up with reserves), meaning each release is a deliberate equal-weight blend of three outstanding years — a practice the house calls its defining winemaking signature. The brand also shattered the assumption that prestige Champagne must be rooted in aristocratic European ownership: Jay-Z's 2014 acquisition made it the first major prestige cuvée owned outright by a hip-hop artist, and the subsequent 2021 LVMH joint venture validated its quality credentials to the traditional trade.

parent_region: Isle of Arran, Islands region, Scotland (Lochranza Distillery in the north; sister distillery Lagg in the south)
description_short_en: Arran was one of the first 'new wave' craft distilleries in Scotland, reviving legal distillation on the island after a 160-year gap when it opened in 1995 — and it did so by defying the island-whisky convention of heavy peat, instead…
description_full_en: Founded 1994 (company incorporated) / 1995 (first spirit) by Harold Currie, former Managing Director of Chivas Brothers. Owner: Isle of Arran Distillers Ltd. — privately held by a group of independent shareholders (no corporate parent); Managing Director: Euan Mitchell. Region: Isle of Arran, Islands region, Scotland (Lochranza Distillery in the north; sister distillery Lagg in the south). Classification: Single Malt Scotch Whisky (Islands). Also produces blended Scotch and a cream liqueur (Arran Gold). No formal classification system applies.. Style: Arran's Lochranza distillery produces an unpeated, 'Highland-style' island malt characterised by bright citrus (orange, lime), green orchard fruit (apple, pear), light cereal crunchiness, and a creamy, gentle mouthfeel — markedly less maritime and smoky than Islay or Talisker peers. The house deliberately avoids caramel colouring and chill filtration across virtually its entire range, and pairs its clean, sweet new make with an unusually wide spectrum of cask types — from ex-bourbon and ex-sherry to Amarone, Sauternes, Port, and Calvados finishes — giving the portfolio exceptional breadth for a small independent. Site: Lochranza Distillery, Glen Eason Biorach ('Valley of the Sharp Waterfalls'), Lochranza, north-west Isle of Arran, Firth of Clyde. Water source: Loch na Davie, high in the granite mountains above the distillery; water cascades through six waterfalls (burn name: Easan Biorach), purified by granite and softened by peat. Microclimate: warm maritime, influenced by the Gulf Stream, with sea breezes and clear mountain air — conditions cited as accelerating maturation. Geology: Highland Boundary Fault bisects the island; northern distillery sits on granite-dominant terrain. Maturation in dunnage, racked, and palletised warehouses on-site (earth-floored dunnage warehouses historically used; some casks previously stored off-site at Springbank, Campbeltown). Vinification: Mash tun: 2.5-tonne semi-lauter type with copper canopy. Six Oregon pine washbacks. Yeast: Kerry M strain. Fermentation: 60–75 hours (longer fermentation targeted to develop fruity congeners). Double distillation in copper pot stills: 2 wash stills (broad pot, tall neck, long lyne arm; 7,100 L each) + 2 spirit stills (bulb-shaped, long lyne arm; 4,300–4,800 L each); all steam-heated with shell-and-tube condensers. New make collected at ~68% ABV. Barley: all-Scottish (Concerto variety cited by distillery). Aging: Primarily ex-bourbon American oak and ex-sherry European oak casks; extended range includes ex-Amarone, ex-Port, ex-Sauternes, ex-Calvados, ex-Cognac, ex-Marsala, and ex-Margaux wine casks for limited finishes. Core age statements: 10, 14, 18, and 21 years. No-age-statement expressions also in core range (Barrel Reserve, Quarter Cask 'The Bothy', Sherry Cask 'The Bodega'). New/used wood split not publicly specified — predominantly refill/used casks for primary maturation, with fresh casks used for finishing. Bottling: No artificial colouring (E150a); non-chill filtered across the core range. Standard bottling strength: 46% ABV. Cask-strength expressions also released (e.g. Quarter Cask 'The Bothy' at 56.2% ABV natural cask strength). Cream liqueur (Arran Gold) bottled separately. Blend/recipe: 100% malted barley (single malt Scotch). Barley variety: Concerto (as stated by distillery). No peat used in standard Lochranza production; peated expressions (Machrie Moor, ~15–20 ppm phenol) produced in small quantities at Lochranza and now primarily at sister distillery Lagg. Grain bill for blended expressions (Robert Burns Blend, Lochranza Blend) not publicly disclosed. Must know: Arran was one of the first 'new wave' craft distilleries in Scotland, reviving legal distillation on the island after a 160-year gap when it opened in 1995 — and it did so by defying the island-whisky convention of heavy peat, instead building its reputation on a clean, fruit-forward, unpeated Highland style. Unusually for an island producer, Arran bottles the vast majority of its range without chill filtration or caramel colouring at 46% ABV or above, and its pioneering use of exotic wine-cask finishes (Amarone, Margaux, Grand Cru Champagne) in the early 2000s helped normalise that practice across the wider Scotch industry.

description_full_en: Founded Estate (Domaine de Bordeneuve / Château de Bordeneuve) traces to the 12th century; Guasch family presence in Gascony documented from that period. Formal commercial Armagnac production date not precisely established in available sources.. Owner: Guasch family (independent; run by father-and-son Jean-Claude and Thomas Guasch). Region: Bas-Armagnac AOC, Gascony, South-West France — centred on Eauze, the historic capital of Armagnac. Classification: Bas-Armagnac AOC (the most prestigious of the three Armagnac sub-appellations, known for its tawny sandy soils and fruit-forward character). Style: Baron de Sigognac is defined by an unusually fruit-forward, rounded profile — roundness and persistent fruit presence married with supple structure and long finishes — that distinguishes it from the more austere, tannic style of many Ténarèze producers. The house's commitment to small-batch, hand-blended expressions and an extraordinary vintage library stretching back to 1893 gives it a depth of character rare even within Bas-Armagnac. Site: Château de Bordeneuve, situated on 42–60 hectares (sources vary; 42 ha cited by the producer's own site and multiple retailers, 60 ha cited by Master of Malt) of prime Bas-Armagnac terroir near Eauze. Soils are tawny-sandy ('sables fauves'), the classic Bas-Armagnac substrate that favours elegant, fruit-driven distillates. The estate's medieval cellars — some with beaten-earth floors — provide a stable microclimate for long-term maturation. The in-house copper alembic still dates to 1921. Vinification: Grapes are fermented with natural yeasts prior to distillation. Distillation is carried out on-site using a traditional Armagnac copper column alembic (continuous still / alembic armagnacais), producing a single-pass distillate at approximately 56% ABV — lower than Cognac's double-distillation output, preserving more congeners and aromatic complexity. Aging: Aged in French oak barrels (Limousin and Vosges forest oak cited for vintage expressions) of traditional Armagnac size (400–420 litres). Each vintage year is matured separately. Age-statement expressions range from VS (minimum 3 years) through 10-year and 20-year releases; vintage single-estate bottlings are held in the estate's 'Paradise' cellar until peak maturity, with the oldest reserves dating to 1893. No new-wood percentage specified in available sources. Bottling: Bottled at 40% ABV for standard age-statement and vintage expressions (e.g. 20 Ans d'Âge). Vintage bottles are prepared to order, allowing the spirit to continue maturing until the last possible moment before release. Filtration and fining details not specified in available sources. Blend/recipe: 50% Ugni Blanc / 50% Baco (as stated on the producer's official product sheets for the 20 Ans d'Âge). The Baco grape — a hybrid variety unique to Armagnac — contributes the house's characteristic richness and fruit depth; Ugni Blanc adds freshness and acidity. Must know: Baron de Sigognac is one of the very few Armagnac estates to own and control every stage of production — vineyards, distillery, ageing cellars, and bottling line — under one roof, a rarity in the region. The estate's use of Baco, a hybrid grape now largely restricted to Armagnac, is a deliberate stylistic choice that sets its fruit profile apart from Cognac and from Armagnac houses that have shifted to all-Ugni Blanc.

parent_region: Rutherford AVA, Napa Valley, California, USA. Estate vineyards also extend into St. Helena, Calistoga, and Carneros AVAs.
description_short_en: BV is the birthplace of Napa Valley's first 'cult' Cabernet: the Georges de Latour Private Reserve, whose inaugural 1936 vintage was identified and championed by André Tchelistcheff — the émigré winemaker who simultaneously introduced cold…
description_full_en: Founded 1900 by Georges de Latour (and his wife Fernande de Latour). Owner: Treasury Wine Estates (TWE), Australia — acquired 2016 from Diageo plc. Region: Rutherford AVA, Napa Valley, California, USA. Estate vineyards also extend into St. Helena, Calistoga, and Carneros AVAs.. Classification: No formal French-style classification. Widely recognised as Napa Valley's first 'cult' Cabernet producer; founding member of the Rutherford Dust Society.. Style: BV's house style is anchored in the so-called 'Rutherford Dust' character — a fine, cocoa-powder tannin texture unique to the western Rutherford Bench — producing full-bodied, structured Cabernet Sauvignons with dark cassis, graphite, wild sage, and gravelly earth notes. Unlike many modern Napa producers who chase opulence, BV balances density with restraint and a Bordeaux-like sense of terroir fidelity, a tradition codified by legendary winemaker André Tchelistcheff from 1938 onward. The flagship Georges de Latour Private Reserve is built for multi-decade cellaring yet shows polished, integrated tannins even in youth. Site: Core sites are BV Ranch No. 1 (originally purchased 1903–1904, 127 acres) and BV Ranch No. 2 on the western bench of the Rutherford AVA, at the foothills of the Mayacamas Mountains. Soils are well-draining alluvial fan deposits from the Mayacamas Mountains — a diverse mix of gravel, sand, and loam — that force deep root penetration and yield small, intensely flavoured berries. BV Ranch No. 2 is 140 acres planted predominantly to Cabernet Sauvignon and benefits from regular morning fog for a long, even growing season. Total Rutherford holdings exceed 360 acres. Additional vineyards (BV9–BV12) extend from Carneros to Calistoga. No specific elevation data confirmed across sources. Vinification: For the Georges de Latour Private Reserve, winemakers choose from three fermentation vessels: stainless steel tanks (2,000-gallon) for bright fruit; 1,600-gallon French oak tanks for tannin integration; and 60-gallon new French oak barrels (400+) for complexity. Fermentation is long and slow — up to 60 days per lot — with gentle hourly five-minute pump-overs for cap management. Cold soak prior to fermentation (7 days documented for Rutherford Reserve tier). Malolactic fermentation follows pressing, conducted in barrel. Fruit is hand-picked and optically sorted at the winery. Aging: Georges de Latour Private Reserve: aged in new French oak barrels (750 barrels documented); aging duration approximately 18–21 months depending on vintage. Rutherford Reserve tier: 21 months in 80–100% new French oak. Reserve Ranch 2: 19 months in 90% new French oak. Specific cooperages are limited and carefully selected for style consistency. Bottling: No confirmed data on fining or filtration status. Wines are bottled at standard ABV (typically ~14.5% for GDL Private Reserve); not cask strength. Chill-filtration status not confirmed in sources reviewed. Blend/recipe: Georges de Latour Private Reserve: predominantly Cabernet Sauvignon with a small addition of Petit Verdot (documented at ~9% in 2022 vintage) for inky colour and violet/floral accents. Clonal selections 4, 6, 7, 8, and 169 from BV Ranches No. 1 and No. 2. Rutherford Reserve: ~85% Cabernet Sauvignon, ~10% Petit Verdot, ~5% Malbec (2019 vintage). Exact varietal percentages vary by vintage. Must know: BV is the birthplace of Napa Valley's first 'cult' Cabernet: the Georges de Latour Private Reserve, whose inaugural 1936 vintage was identified and championed by André Tchelistcheff — the émigré winemaker who simultaneously introduced cold fermentation, malolactic fermentation, and small French oak barrel aging to California, techniques now universal across the industry. The 2021 GDL Private Reserve was named Wine Spectator #2 Wine of the Year 2024, confirming the estate's continued relevance at the very top of American Cabernet.

parent_region: Champagne AOC, headquartered in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ, Vallée de la Marne, near Épernay; grapes sourced across Montagne de Reims, Côte des Blancs, and Vallée de la Marne
description_short_en: Billecart-Salmon pioneered the modern prestige rosé Champagne category: their Brut Rosé NV (relaunched 1970s) and Cuvée Elisabeth Salmon (1988) set the benchmark for pale, Chardonnay-dominant rosé Champagne at a time when rosé was…
description_full_en: Founded 1818 by Nicolas François Billecart and Elisabeth Salmon (with Louis Salmon, Elisabeth's brother, as founding winemaker). Owner: Roland-Billecart family (majority, 55%) with minority stake (45%) held by the Frey Group (owners of Château La Lagune, Paul Jaboulet Ainé, and Château Corton C); CEO is 7th-generation Mathieu Roland-Billecart (since 2019). Region: Champagne AOC, headquartered in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ, Vallée de la Marne, near Épernay; grapes sourced across Montagne de Reims, Côte des Blancs, and Vallée de la Marne. Classification: Champagne AOC (Grande Marque / Négociant-Manipulant); no official Champagne classification system exists, but universally regarded as a prestige Grande Marque. Style: Billecart-Salmon is defined by crystalline freshness, fine-boned elegance, and gastronomic precision — a deliberate counterpoint to the richer, more oxidative styles of many grandes maisons. The house's hallmark double cold-settling and low-temperature fermentation (~13°C over 3–4 weeks) produce wines of exceptional aromatic purity and tension. Extended lees aging — well beyond legal minimums — adds textural complexity without sacrificing the house's signature lift and balance. Site: Estate centered in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ, south of the Montagne de Reims, on chalk-dominant soils typical of the Champagne heartland. The house directly manages ~100 hectares and sources from a total of ~300 hectares across 40 crus within a 20 km radius of Épernay, spanning Montagne de Reims (Pinot Noir), Côte des Blancs (Chardonnay — Grand Crus: Avize, Cramant, Chouilly, Oger, Le Mesnil-sur-Oger), and Vallée de la Marne (Pinot Meunier). The flagship single-hectare monopole Clos Saint-Hilaire (formerly the family's tennis courts) is located in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ, farmed biodynamically with horse ploughing and sheep grazing; 10 ha total biodynamic, 100 ha organic as of 2025. Cellars are 17th-century chalk tunnels (~2 km long) providing constant cool temperatures. Vinification: Primary fermentation in 150 individual 40-hl thermoregulated stainless-steel tanks (installed 2000), allowing parcel-by-parcel vinification to preserve terroir nuance. Signature technique: double cold-settling of must followed by extended low-temperature fermentation at ~13°C for 3–4 weeks (vs. industry norm of 2–3 days), inspired by traditional brewing methods introduced by Jean Roland-Billecart in 1958. Malolactic fermentation is not systematic — decided tank-by-tank after alcoholic fermentation based on vintage, parcel, and variety; often blocked for prestige cuvées to preserve tension. A dedicated oak chai (opened 2010) holds 450+ barrels (average age ~15–20 vintages, sourced from Burgundy including ex-Domaine Leflaive); also uses 80-hl thermoregulated foudres. Prestige and Sous Bois cuvées see partial or full barrel vinification. Aging: Non-vintage cuvées: minimum 3 years on lees in chalk cellars (Le Réserve currently averages ~50 months); vintage prestige cuvées: minimum 10 years on lees (Clos Saint-Hilaire 2009 aged 177 months; Elisabeth Salmon aged ~115 months). Oak used for Sous Bois (100% old Burgundian barrels, ~15-year-old average), Louis Salmon (50% oak), Nicolas François (small % barrel, increasing over vintages), and Clos Saint-Hilaire (100% oak). No new oak used — barrels average 15–20 vintages old to impart micro-oxygenation without overt wood flavour. Bottling: Entire range transitioned to Extra Brut dosage (Le Réserve at 3 g/L; Clos Saint-Hilaire at 1.9 g/L; Elisabeth Salmon at 3.8 g/L). Chill-filtration and fining status not publicly confirmed. ABV typically 12–12.5%. MyOrigin back-label QR code discloses disgorgement date, dosage, reserve wine vintages, and varietal composition per bottle. Blend/recipe: Le Réserve NV: ~40% Pinot Meunier (Vallée de la Marne / Coteaux Sud d'Épernay), ~30% Chardonnay, ~30% Pinot Noir (Montagne de Reims / Grande Vallée de la Marne); 50%+ reserve wines from up to 15 vintages. Brut Rosé NV: 30% Chardonnay, 30% Pinot Noir, 40% Pinot Meunier; ~8–10% still red Pinot Noir from old-vine Valofroy vineyard in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ. Elisabeth Salmon Rosé (vintage): 50% Pinot Noir (Mareuil-sur-Aÿ, Verzenay), 50% Chardonnay (Chouilly, Avize, Le Mesnil-sur-Oger) — Grand and Premier Cru only. Louis Salmon Blanc de Blancs (vintage): 100% Chardonnay from Grand Cru Côte des Blancs (Avize, Cramant, Chouilly, Oger, Le Mesnil-sur-Oger). Clos Saint-Hilaire (vintage): 100% Pinot Noir from single 1-ha monopole. Sous Bois NV: equal thirds Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Meunier — all oak-vinified. Must know: Billecart-Salmon pioneered the modern prestige rosé Champagne category: their Brut Rosé NV (relaunched 1970s) and Cuvée Elisabeth Salmon (1988) set the benchmark for pale, Chardonnay-dominant rosé Champagne at a time when rosé was considered second-class. The house's 1958 introduction of double cold-settling and low-temperature fermentation — borrowed from brewing — remains its most distinctive technical signature and is the primary reason its wines age more gracefully than most peers; the Cuvée Nicolas François 1959 was voted 'Champagne of the Millennium' in 1999 from a blind tasting of 150 vintages.

parent_region: Napa Valley AVA, California, USA — winery based in Oakville; five cru vineyards spread across Oakville, Rutherford, St. Helena, Yountville, and Spring Mountain District sub-AVAs
description_short_en: BOND is structurally unique in Napa: it owns none of its vineyards, instead holding long-term farming contracts and deploying its own 55-person full-time vineyard crew on third-party land — a model closer to a Burgundian négociant-éleveur…
description_full_en: Founded 1997 by Bill Harlan (H. William Harlan II) and Bob Levy. Owner: Harlan family (Bill Harlan, now managed day-to-day by son Will Harlan since 2020); independent. Region: Napa Valley AVA, California, USA — winery based in Oakville; five cru vineyards spread across Oakville, Rutherford, St. Helena, Yountville, and Spring Mountain District sub-AVAs. Classification: Napa Valley AVA (no formal U.S. grand cru classification); self-designated 'Grand Cru' single-vineyard Cabernet Sauvignon portfolio. Style: BOND applies a Burgundian single-vineyard philosophy to 100% Cabernet Sauvignon, bottling five distinct 'cru' sites separately to express micro-terroir differences across the Napa Valley — a deliberate inversion of the Bordeaux blending model used at sibling estate Harlan Estate. Each wine is vinified identically so that only the character of the site speaks, producing wines that range from the charming, clay-driven red-fruit elegance of Melbury to the monumental volcanic structure of Vecina and the lithic salinity of Quella. Site: Five hillside sites, each 7–11 acres, all farmed by BOND's own in-house vineyard team under long-term contracts with the landowners: (1) Melbury — 400–500 ft elevation, clay/humus soils, south/southeast-facing slopes east of Rutherford above Lake Hennessey; (2) Vecina — 221–330 ft, volcanic bedrock overlain with fine-grained alluvial wash, east-facing, adjacent to BOND winery in Oakville western foothills; (3) St. Eden — 145–188 ft (lowest site), iron-rich red rocky volcanic soils from a Vaca Mountain landslide, north-facing knoll just north of Oakville Crossroad; (4) Quella — 433–595 ft, ancient lifted riverbed of cobble, rocks, and pockets of tufa (volcanic ash), southwest-facing, Spring Valley eastern hills; (5) Pluribus — over 1,000 ft (highest site), volcanic bedrock, Spring Mountain District western slopes. Winery facility located in Oakville, originally the old Harlan Estate winery, dedicated to BOND after the 2000 harvest. Vinification: Spontaneous (native yeast) fermentation, conducted mostly in barrels, at 78–82°F; four pump-overs per day plus punch-downs at peak fermentation; long maceration for maximum natural colour and tannin extraction; post-fermentation maceration length adjusted per vineyard and vintage personality; each harvest block picked separately (one vineyard may be harvested in up to 17 separate passes) and vinified individually Aging: Matured in French oak barrels — almost entirely new oak — from coopers Taransaud, Darnajou, and Sylvain; individual batches aged separately before final blending; specific duration not publicly confirmed but consistent with Napa cult-Cabernet norms of ~18–22 months Bottling: Minimal-intervention philosophy; specific fining/filtration details not publicly disclosed; bottled at natural ABV (typically ~14–15% for Napa hillside Cabernet); not cask-strength Blend/recipe: 100% Cabernet Sauvignon per cru wine; wines are labeled by vineyard name (Melbury, Vecina, St. Eden, Quella, Pluribus) and are sourced overwhelmingly — approximately 90–100% — from the named single vineyard, with flexibility to incorporate small amounts from other BOND sites in challenging vintages. A sixth wine, Matriarch, blends fruit from all five sites and serves as the second wine. Must know: BOND is structurally unique in Napa: it owns none of its vineyards, instead holding long-term farming contracts and deploying its own 55-person full-time vineyard crew on third-party land — a model closer to a Burgundian négociant-éleveur than a Napa estate. A candidate vineyard is typically farmed by BOND for 5–7 years (sometimes over a decade) before its wine is ever commercially released, meaning the portfolio expands only when a site has proven its 'grand cru' character through repeated blind evaluation.

parent_region: Ellon, Aberdeenshire, Scotland (primary brewing HQ); originally founded in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire
description_short_en: BrewDog pioneered the 'Equity for Punks' crowdfunding model in 2010–2011, raising capital directly from beer fans and creating over 200,000 small shareholders — a then-unprecedented approach for a UK brewery that helped fuel its rapid…
description_full_en: Founded 2007 by James Watt and Martin Dickie. Owner: Tilray Brands (acquired UK brewing operations, global brand IP, and 11 brewpubs in March 2026; US and Australian operations acquired separately thereafter). Region: Ellon, Aberdeenshire, Scotland (primary brewing HQ); originally founded in Fraserburgh, Aberdeenshire. Classification: Independent craft brewery (no formal appellation classification); Scotland's largest independent craft brewery prior to 2026 acquisition. Style: BrewDog built its identity on aggressively hopped, high-ABV craft beers that deliberately challenged the conventions of the UK beer market — most notably its flagship Punk IPA, a 5.6% ABV pale ale loaded with New World hops delivering tropical fruit, grapefruit, and a spiky bitter finish. Unlike traditional British ale producers, BrewDog leans heavily on American and Southern Hemisphere hop varieties (Chinook, Ahtanum, Simcoe, Amarillo, Nelson Sauvin, Cascade) and employs post-fermentation dry-hopping at cellar temperature to maximise aromatic intensity. The house ethos spans an unusually wide ABV range — from 0.5% alcohol-free beers to extreme high-strength expressions — making it one of the most stylistically diverse craft producers in the UK. Site: Primary brewery located at Balmacassie Commercial Park, Ellon, Aberdeenshire, AB41 8BX, Scotland. The 5.5-acre site opened in January 2013 at a cost of £7.8 million and was expanded in 2016 with a new 300-hectolitre brewhouse at a cost of £5 million. The facility incorporates biogas technology and (since 2021) an anaerobic digestion plant to reduce carbon emissions. Theoretical capacity is up to 2 million hectolitres per year, though the site was operating at approximately one-third capacity at the time of the 2026 sale. Water source is local Aberdeenshire supply. No vineyard; no single named terroir site. Vinification: All-grain brewing; 60-minute boil standard across core range. Punk IPA uses a multi-addition hop schedule (bittering additions at 60 and 30 minutes, aroma/whirlpool additions at 15 minutes and flameout). Ale yeast fermentation; commercial production uses proprietary yeast strains with continuous yeast management and fermentation profile optimisation. Mash temperature approximately 65°C. Aging: No barrel aging on core range. Specialist expressions (e.g. Paradox series) are aged in Scotch whisky casks; duration varies by release. No standard new/used wood percentage applicable to the core lineup. Bottling: Core beers (e.g. Punk IPA at 5.6% ABV) are filtered and carbonated; not cask strength. Vegan-certified across 23+ SKUs. Dry-hopped post-fermentation at approximately 14°C for 5 days on hop-forward beers. High-ABV extreme releases (e.g. historical 'End of History' at 55% ABV) produced via freeze distillation — not standard brewing. Blend/recipe: Punk IPA (flagship): Maris Otter Extra Pale malt base; hop bill includes Chinook, Ahtanum, Cascade, Simcoe, Amarillo, and Nelson Sauvin — sourced from Yakima Valley (USA) and New Zealand. Malted barley sourced from UK and Germany. ABV 5.6%, IBU approximately 35–40 (commercial spec). Dry-hop charge with six hop varieties post-fermentation. Full recipe publicly available via BrewDog's open-source 'DIY Dog' catalogue. Must know: BrewDog pioneered the 'Equity for Punks' crowdfunding model in 2010–2011, raising capital directly from beer fans and creating over 200,000 small shareholders — a then-unprecedented approach for a UK brewery that helped fuel its rapid international expansion. The brand also open-sourced its entire back catalogue of beer recipes ('DIY Dog'), a radical transparency move that no major craft brewery had attempted at scale, cementing its anti-establishment positioning.

parent_region: Rhinns of Islay, Isle of Islay, Inner Hebrides, Scotland (Islay Single Malt Scotch Whisky)
description_short_en: Bruichladdich is the only Islay distillery that produces the world's most heavily peated whisky (Octomore, 150–309+ ppm) and a completely unpeated flagship in the same stillhouse — breaking the assumption that Islay equals peat. It was…
description_full_en: Founded 1881 by William, John, and Robert Harvey (Harvey brothers, a dynastic Glasgow whisky family). Owner: Rémy Cointreau (acquired July 2012 for £58 million). Region: Rhinns of Islay, Isle of Islay, Inner Hebrides, Scotland (Islay Single Malt Scotch Whisky). Classification: Single Malt Scotch Whisky (Islay); no official grand cru or classified growth equivalent — self-styled 'Progressive Hebridean Distillers'; all output sold exclusively as single malt, never for blending. Style: Bruichladdich is the anomaly of Islay: its flagship range is entirely unpeated, producing a sweet, honeyed, floral and citrus-fresh spirit that stands in deliberate contrast to the island's peat-bomb reputation. Uniquely, the distillery operates three distinct peat-level brands under one roof — unpeated Bruichladdich, heavily peated Port Charlotte (~40 ppm), and super-heavily peated Octomore (150–309+ ppm) — yet all three retain the distillery's inherent freshness and coastal acidity. A radical terroir philosophy drives the house: barley provenance (farm, field, variety, harvest year) is disclosed on every bottle, a level of agricultural transparency virtually unmatched in Scotch whisky. Site: Situated on the western shore of Loch Indaal, Rhinns of Islay, at near sea level. The site was chosen in 1881 for its gentle slope, which enables fully gravity-fed production with no pumps. Mash/process water drawn from An Torran (Bruichladdich loch reservoir — soft and peaty); reduction/bottling water from the Octomore Farm spring (~2 miles away), a high-silica source flowing through ancient Lewisian gneiss rock, unique on the island. Cooling water from the Bruichladdich burn. Climate is Atlantic-maritime: washed by the Gulf Stream, rarely freezing, with high rainfall and humidity — conditions that slow maturation in the flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses. All maturation takes place on Islay. Vinification: Grain milled on the last surviving belt-driven Robert Bobby Mill (refurbished 2001). Mashing in the original 1881 seven-tonne cast-iron open rake-and-plough mash tun — the largest of only three such tuns still operating in Scotland. Wort fermented in six Douglas Fir (Oregon Pine) wooden washbacks (combined 210,000 litres); fermentation is deliberately slow, with wooden vessels considered integral to flavour development. No computers used in production — entirely hand-operated and gravity-fed. Double distillation in two wash stills (~12,500 litres each) and two unusually tall (6-metre), narrow-necked (0.9m) spirit stills (~10,500 litres each), all indirect steam-heated with shell-and-tube condensers; tall narrow necks maximise copper contact and reflux for a clean, elegant new make. A fifth still — 'Ugly Betty', an 11,600-litre Lomond pot-still salvaged from Inverleven distillery (installed 2010) — provides adjustable reflux for experimental distillates and The Botanist gin. Spirit cut points approximately 70% down to 63% ABV. Aging: Exclusively on Islay in flat, brick-built dunnage warehouses; Atlantic-maritime climate ensures slow, gradual maturation. Cask policy is deliberately diverse: primarily ex-Bourbon barrels, with significant use of French wine casks (including first-growth Bordeaux), sherry-seasoned oak, and other fortified wine casks. No standard new/used wood percentage disclosed — varies by expression and batch. The Classic Laddie (NAS) is a multi-vintage assemblage curated by the Head Distiller. Aged expressions include 18-year-old (primarily ex-Bourbon, bottled at 50% ABV) and 30-year-old (exclusively ex-Bourbon, bottled at 43% ABV). Bottling: No chill-filtration; no caramel colouring added — across all expressions. Bottled on Islay at the distillery's own commercially scaled bottling hall (installed 2003), using Octomore Farm spring water for reduction. ABV varies by expression: Classic Laddie at 50%, Bruichladdich 18 at 50%, Bruichladdich 30 at 43%; Octomore releases typically cask strength (varies per edition, often 59–61% ABV). Bottles use ~60% recycled glass. Blend/recipe: 100% Scottish malted barley — no grain whisky. Over 50% of barley now grown by 20 local Islay farming partners (programme started 2004); remainder sourced from Scottish mainland maltings. Barley varieties include standard distilling varieties, certified organic barley, and heritage Bere barley for special editions. Peating levels: Bruichladdich = 0 ppm (unpeated); Port Charlotte = ~40 ppm; Octomore = 150–309+ ppm (varies by edition — Octomore 08.3 reached 309 ppm). Peated malting for Port Charlotte and Octomore carried out off-island at Bairds Malt, Inverness. Must know: Bruichladdich is the only Islay distillery that produces the world's most heavily peated whisky (Octomore, 150–309+ ppm) and a completely unpeated flagship in the same stillhouse — breaking the assumption that Islay equals peat. It was also the first Scotch whisky distillery to achieve B Corp certification (2020) and the first to grow barley commercially on Islay in the modern era (2004), pioneering a terroir-in-whisky concept borrowed from fine wine that has since influenced the wider industry.

description_short_en: Casanova di Neri was the first Italian wine to receive a perfect 100-point score from Wine Spectator (2001 Cerretalto, awarded 2007), and the 2001 Tenuta Nuova was named Wine Spectator Wine of the Year in 2006 — a watershed moment that put…
description_full_en: Founded 1971 by Giovanni Neri. Owner: Neri family (independent; currently led by Giacomo Neri, 2nd generation, with 3rd-generation sons Giovanni and Gianlorenzo Neri). Region: Brunello di Montalcino DOCG, Montalcino, Tuscany, Italy. Classification: Brunello di Montalcino DOCG (primary); Rosso di Montalcino DOC; Toscana IGT (Pietradonice Cabernet Sauvignon). Style: Casanova di Neri represents a modern-leaning interpretation of Brunello di Montalcino, characterised by rich dark fruit, fine polished tannins, and refined oak integration rather than the austere, tannic profile of traditional producers. The estate's multi-vineyard approach — spanning both the cooler north-eastern and warmer southern sectors of Montalcino — allows it to produce wines of both power (Tenuta Nuova) and mineral precision (Cerretalto). Under the third generation, the house style has evolved toward less extraction and greater fruit balance, while retaining exceptional aging potential. Site: Seven vineyards totalling ~63–79 ha across Montalcino: (1) Cerretalto — 270–300 m a.s.l., east-facing, galestro marl with alluvial stone and eroded rock rich in iron, magnesium, and plinthites; situated in a natural amphitheatre along the Asso torrent in Torrenieri; widest diurnal temperature range of all estate sites; white truffles grow on-site. (2) Fiesole — 350–380 m a.s.l., south-east-facing, galestro; adjacent to winery HQ in north-east Montalcino. (3) Tenuta Nuova / Le Cetine — southern Montalcino near Castelnuovo dell'Abate; first Sangiovese planting in that zone. (4) Podernuovo — highest vineyard at ~450–480 m a.s.l., Torrenieri; chestnut-fringed, galestro. (5) Pietradonice — 250–300 m a.s.l., south-east, galestro marl rich in onyx fragments; former onyx quarry near Abbey of Sant'Antimo. (6) Giovanni Neri — 320–390 m a.s.l., south-facing, galestro marl with clay and tufa; 50-year-old vines. (7) Collalli. All vineyards planted with a proprietary Sangiovese Grosso clone selected from Cerretalto. Vinification: Manual bunch selection on a sorting belt, followed by destemming; individual berry selection via optical sorter. Gravity-fed (no pumps) into open-topped truncated conical vats of stainless steel and wood. Spontaneous fermentation with no added yeasts; maceration with frequent punch-downs at controlled temperature for approximately 23–24 days (varies by cuvée). Three-story gravity-fed cellar built into the hillside maintains natural temperature and humidity. Aging: Varies by label: White Label Brunello — large botte and tonneaux, ~43 months total oak; Tenuta Nuova — tonneaux, ~30 months; Cerretalto — fermented in oak, aged in tonneaux (20% new oak), ~30 months in barrel plus ~30 months in bottle (released one year after stablemates); Rosso di Montalcino — 12 months in wood. Burgundian-influenced slow barrel aging in two dedicated barrel rooms. Bottling: No specific fining/filtration data confirmed publicly. ABV varies: Tenuta Nuova 2018 noted at 15%; Cerretalto typically ~14.5%. Not cask strength. Blend/recipe: Brunello di Montalcino (all labels): 100% Sangiovese Grosso (Brunello clone proprietary to estate, selected from Cerretalto). Pietradonice (IGT): 100% Cabernet Sauvignon from Pietradonice vineyard. IrROSSO (IGT): Sangiovese-dominant blend with small additions of other estate varieties. IBBianco (IGT): Vermentino and Grechetto. Must know: Casanova di Neri was the first Italian wine to receive a perfect 100-point score from Wine Spectator (2001 Cerretalto, awarded 2007), and the 2001 Tenuta Nuova was named Wine Spectator Wine of the Year in 2006 — a watershed moment that put single-vineyard Brunello on the global map. Uniquely, the estate propagates its own proprietary Sangiovese Grosso clone bred from the Cerretalto vineyard and plants it across all seven sites, creating a coherent house DNA while expressing radically different terroir expressions across north-east and southern Montalcino.

parent_region: Vallée de la Marne AOC, Champagne — right bank (Rive Droite), villages of Châtillon-sur-Marne and Vandières, western Marne Valley
description_short_en: Fabien Cazé was admitted into the elite 'Terres et Vins de Champagne' collective (alongside Bérêche, Agrapart, and Chartogne-Taillet) after only his second release — an extraordinary recognition for a producer with fewer than 20,000…
description_full_en: Founded 1953 by Roger Cazé (first in the family to commercially estate-bottle Champagne; family viticulture spans 10 generations). Owner: Cazé family — independent; currently led by Fabien Cazé (10th generation), grandson of Roger Cazé. Region: Vallée de la Marne AOC, Champagne — right bank (Rive Droite), villages of Châtillon-sur-Marne and Vandières, western Marne Valley. Classification: AOC Champagne — Récoltant-Manipulant (grower Champagne); no Premier Cru or Grand Cru village classification (Châtillon-sur-Marne and Vandières are unclassified villages). Note: a Cramant Grand Cru cuvée exists in the range, sourced from the Grand Cru village of Cramant.. Style: Cazé-Thibaut produces hyper-parcel-specific, terroir-transparent Champagnes from a tiny 2.66-hectare holding, with an emphasis on Pinot Meunier — the dominant grape of the western Marne Valley — alongside Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. The house style is defined by precision, tension, and mineral purity, achieved through slow cold native-yeast fermentations, no malolactic conversion, and extended lees aging (24–72 months), resulting in wines that are straight, bright, and vinous rather than creamy or toasty. Unlike most grower Champagnes, Fabien deliberately avoids MLF and uses very low dosage (typically Extra Brut, under 3 g/L), placing him firmly in the new-wave artisan camp alongside Bérêche and Chartogne-Taillet. Site: Six distinct parcels totalling 2.66 ha on the right bank of the Marne River, split between Châtillon-sur-Marne (east-facing slopes) and Vandières (south-facing slopes). Soils are clay-limestone with sandy clay topsoil over a chalk bedrock that lies close to the surface on high hillside positions. The underground winemaking cellar — a 19th-century vaulted chalk corridor on rue des Crayères in Venteuil, acquired in 2022 — provides naturally cold, stable temperatures that inhibit malolactic fermentation. Pressing takes place at a shared Coquard automatic inclined-plate press in Châtillon-sur-Marne; juice is gravity-transferred to the cellar. Vinification: Grapes are hand-harvested parcel by parcel at optimal ripeness and pressed slowly and gently in a Coquard inclined-plate press. Clarified juice is gravity-fed into stainless-steel tanks, then approximately half is transferred into a combination of 228 L, 300 L, 330 L, and 350 L oak barrels for spontaneous (native yeast) alcoholic fermentation — long, cold, and slow in the underground chalk cellar. Malolactic fermentation is not sought and rarely completes due to the cellar's low ambient temperatures. Aging: Extended aging on fine lees: 24 to 72 months en tirage depending on the cuvée. Barrels used are a mix of sizes (228–350 L); new-oak percentage not publicly specified but the approach is low-intervention with used/neutral wood dominant. Parcellaire cuvées receive longer tirage than the entry-level 'Naturellement'. Bottling: No fining, no filtration. Dosage is determined by blind tasting just prior to disgorgement. Typically Extra Brut (dosage 1.7–2.7 g/L depending on cuvée); the 'Naturellement' Blanc de Noirs is released as Brut Nature or Extra Brut at ≤2 g/L. ABV not uniformly published across cuvées. Blend/recipe: Range is parcel-specific and varies by cuvée: 'Naturellement' = 100% Pinot Meunier; 'Les Leriens' = ~45% Chardonnay / 45% Pinot Meunier / 10% Pinot Noir (dosage ~2.7 g/L); 'Le Chemin des Plants' = 100% Chardonnay (Vandières); 'Cramant Grand Cru' = 100% Chardonnay (Cramant); 'Jossias Rosé' = blend including Pinot Meunier. Pinot Meunier is the backbone of the estate given its Marne Valley location. Must know: Fabien Cazé was admitted into the elite 'Terres et Vins de Champagne' collective (alongside Bérêche, Agrapart, and Chartogne-Taillet) after only his second release — an extraordinary recognition for a producer with fewer than 20,000 bottles of annual production. His deliberate avoidance of malolactic fermentation and use of very low dosage in a region historically dominated by Pinot Meunier-based blended NV Champagnes marks him as a rule-breaker who treats the western Marne Valley with the same parcel-level seriousness usually reserved for the Côte des Blancs.

Unlike most grower Champagnes, Fabien deliberately avoids MLF and uses very low dosage (typically Extra Brut, under 3 g/L), placing him firmly in the new-wave artisan camp alongside Bérêche and Chartogne-Taillet.", "vineyard_or_distillery": "Six distinct parcels totalling 2.66 ha on the right bank of the Marne River, split between Châtillon-sur-Marne (east-facing slopes) and Vandières (south-facing slopes). Soils are clay-limestone with sandy clay topsoil over a chalk bedrock that lies close to the surface on high hillside positions. The underground winemaking cellar — a 19th-century vaulted chalk corridor on rue des Crayères in Venteuil, acquired in 2022 — provides naturally cold, stable temperatures that inhibit malolactic fermentation. Pressing takes place at a shared Coquard automatic inclined-plate press in Châtillon-sur-Marne; juice is gravity-transferred to the cellar.", "winemaking_or_production": {"vinification": "Grapes are hand-harvested parcel by parcel at optimal ripeness and pressed slowly and gently in a Coquard inclined-plate press. Clarified juice is gravity-fed into stainless-steel tanks, then approximately half is transferred into a combination of 228 L, 300 L, 330 L, and 350 L oak barrels for spontaneous (native yeast) alcoholic fermentation — long, cold, and slow in the underground chalk cellar. Malolactic fermentation is not sought and rarely completes due to the cellar's low ambient temperatures.", "aging": "Extended aging on fine lees: 24 to 72 months en tirage depending on the cuvée. Barrels used are a mix of sizes (228–350 L); new-oak percentage not publicly specified but the approach is low-intervention with used/neutral wood dominant. Parcellaire cuvées receive longer tirage than the entry-level 'Naturellement'.", "bottling": "No fining, no filtration. Dosage is determined by blind tasting just prior to disgorgement. Typically Extra Brut (dosage 1.7–2.7 g/L depending on cuvée); the 'Naturellement' Blanc de Noirs is released as Brut Nature or Extra Brut at ≤2 g/L. Pinot Meunier is the backbone of the estate given its Marne Valley location.", "must_know": "Fabien Cazé was admitted into the elite 'Terres et Vins de Champagne' collective (alongside Bérêche, Agrapart, and Chartogne-Taillet) after only his second release — an extraordinary recognition for a producer with fewer than 20,000 bottles of annual production. His deliberate avoidance of malolactic fermentation and use of very low dosage in a region historically dominated by Pinot Meunier-based blended NV Champagnes marks him as a rule-breaker who treats the western Marne Valley with the same parcel-level seriousness usually reserved for the Côte des Blancs.", "vintage_notes": {"2020": "2020: warm, dry growing season producing ripe, concentrated fruit with good natural acidity; Cazé-Thibaut's 2020 parcellaires (Le Chemin des Plants, Les Leriens) received 95-point scores from Antonio Galloni (Vinous) for their sculpted tension and mineral precision.", "2021": "2021: challenging, cool and uneven season in Champagne requiring rigorous selection; parent_region: Côteaux Sud d'Épernay, Marne, Champagne AOC — village of Chavot-Courcourt, south of Épernay
description_short_en: Chavost is one of the only Champagne cooperatives in the region to have converted its entire portfolio to zero-sulphur-added, zero-dosage, organically farmed production — a feat achieved by chef de caves Fabian Daviaux, who persuaded 70+…
description_full_en: Founded 1946 by a collective of approximately 20 winegrower families from Chavot-Courcourt (founder names not individually documented); natural-wine label launched 2019 under chef de caves Fabian Daviaux, great-grandson of a founding member. Owner: Independent grower cooperative (over 70 multi-generational member families as of 2019–2023; no external corporate owner identified). Region: Côteaux Sud d'Épernay, Marne, Champagne AOC — village of Chavot-Courcourt, south of Épernay. Classification: Champagne AOC (non-vintage Brut Nature / Extra Brut); also produces Coteaux Champenois AOC still wine. No Premier or Grand Cru village classification — Chavot-Courcourt is unclassified.. Style: Chavost is one of Champagne's only cooperatives to produce an entirely zero-sulphur-added, zero-dosage (Brut Nature) portfolio using certified organic fruit, native-yeast fermentation, and no fining or filtration — a radical departure from the additive-heavy norms of co-op Champagne. The wines are single-vintage in character (though not officially vintage-labeled due to short tirage time) and emphasise fresh, linear, acid-driven fruit over the rich, toasty autolytic style typical of larger houses. Pinot Meunier — often undervalued by grandes maisons — is a signature grape here, lending texture and generosity to the assemblage. Site: 5–5.5 hectares across 22 plots in Chavot-Courcourt, certified organic from harvest 2023 (conversion began 2019). Soils are clay-limestone (north-facing aspect per technical sheets), with patches of chalky-limestone also present — the dual soil mosaic sits at the geographic junction of the Côte de Blancs and the Vallée de la Marne. Vines are 35–60 years old. Manual harvest at optimal maturity. Vinification: 100% first-press cuvée juice only (taille excluded). Base wines fermented in thermoregulated stainless steel vats. Spontaneous alcoholic fermentation with indigenous yeasts at 17°C; spontaneous malolactic fermentation with indigenous bacteria at 20°C. No enzymes, no tannin additions, no coal fining, no chaptalization, no acidification or deacidification, no added sulphites at any stage. Second fermentation in bottle (méthode traditionnelle) without added sugar — no liqueur de tirage sugar addition. Aging: Minimum ~20 months on lees in bottle (per distributor notes); Coteaux Champenois still wine aged 18 months in used French oak barrels. No new oak used for sparkling cuvées. Bottling: Unfiltered, unfined. Zero dosage (Brut Nature) across most cuvées; Blanc de Chardonnay labeled Extra Brut (also zero or near-zero dosage). No added sulphites — naturally occurring SO₂ only (measured at 6–18 mg/L total depending on cuvée). ABV not confirmed across all cuvées; typical Champagne range ~12–12.5% assumed but not verified from primary source. Blend/recipe: Blanc d'Assemblage Brut Nature (flagship, ~9,000 bottles): 47% Chardonnay / 47% Pinot Meunier / 6% Pinot Noir. Blanc de Meunier Brut Nature: 100% Pinot Meunier. Blanc de Chardonnay Extra Brut: 100% Chardonnay. Rosé de Saignée Brut Nature: ~67% Pinot Meunier / ~33% Pinot Noir (~1,000 bottles). Eurêka! Brut Nature: 70% Pinot Noir / 15% Chardonnay / 15% Pinot Meunier (~2,000 bottles). Coteaux Champenois: 100% Chardonnay (~1,000–1,182 bottles). Must know: Chavost is one of the only Champagne cooperatives in the region to have converted its entire portfolio to zero-sulphur-added, zero-dosage, organically farmed production — a feat achieved by chef de caves Fabian Daviaux, who persuaded 70+ co-op members to go organic starting in 2019. All wines are technically single-vintage but carry no vintage date on the label, as short tirage times preclude official vintage status under Champagne AOC rules.

parent_region: Saint-Émilion AOC, Right Bank Bordeaux, France — situated at the far north-western edge of Saint-Émilion, directly bordering Pomerol
description_short_en: Cheval Blanc is the only Bordeaux First Growth-equivalent that breaks the Right Bank rule of Merlot dominance — Cabernet Franc ('Bouchet') has been the estate's structural backbone since Jean Laussac-Fourcaud's replanting in the 1860s, a…
description_full_en: Founded 1832 by Jean-Jacques Ducasse (estate formally shaped under son-in-law Jean Laussac-Fourcaud from 1852; first sold under the Cheval Blanc name 1852). Owner: LVMH (Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton) — Bernard Arnault and Belgian businessman Baron Albert Frère acquired the estate in 1998; LVMH absorbed Arnault's personal share in 2009. Current Director: Arnaud de Laforcade; Technical Director: Pierre-Olivier Clouet.. Region: Saint-Émilion AOC, Right Bank Bordeaux, France — situated at the far north-western edge of Saint-Émilion, directly bordering Pomerol. Classification: Formerly Premier Grand Cru Classé A (Saint-Émilion, 1954–2021). In 2021 the estate voluntarily withdrew from the classification, citing lack of transparency and criteria unrelated to wine quality (social-media presence, tourism). Now labelled Saint-Émilion Grand Cru. Widely regarded as a de facto Bordeaux First Growth.. Style: Cheval Blanc is the only major Right Bank estate where Cabernet Franc — not Merlot — is the dominant variety, giving the Grand Vin its hallmark floral perfume (violets, roses), silky tannin architecture, and extraordinary longevity that sets it apart from Pomerol's Merlot-driven richness and Saint-Émilion's limestone-plateau style. Its borderline position between Saint-Émilion and Pomerol produces a wine that combines the finesse of the former with the plush depth of the latter, a duality no other estate can replicate. The house philosophy deliberately harvests some parcels 'al dente' — before full phenolic ripeness — blending different ripeness levels across 56 plots to achieve complexity, freshness, and tension rather than sheer power. Site: 42 hectares (39 planted) divided into 56 plots, in one contiguous block on quaternary alluvium from the Isle river — non-calcareous soils that differ fundamentally from the limestone plateau typical of Saint-Émilion. Three distinct soil types: ~40% gravel over clay (including deep blue clay in front of the château, home to the oldest Cabernet Franc vines); ~40% deep gravel; ~20% sandy clay. The vineyard is considered to have three terroir identities: one-third Pomerol character (border proximity), one-third Graves (gravelly soils), one-third classic Saint-Émilion. Vine density 8,000 vines/ha; average vine age ~45 years; some Cabernet Franc parcels planted 1920 (over 100 years old), used as the massal selection source. Microclimate is slightly warmer and more precocious than surrounding Saint-Émilion, aiding Cabernet Franc ripening. No elevation data published; estate is essentially flat alluvial terrain. Winery (2011) designed by architect Christian de Portzamparc. Vinification: Each of the 56 plots is vinified separately in one of 52–67 temperature-controlled concrete vats (curved, funnel-shaped, 14 different sizes) sized to match individual plot tonnage; filled by gravity to two-thirds capacity to avoid pumping. Destemmed berries transported in 450 kg funnel vats. Malolactic fermentation in tank. After ~3 months, 15–35 selected parcels are assembled for the first blending trial; final blend decided solely by tasting, with no prescriptive varietal ratio. Aging: 16–18 months in 100% new French oak barrels (225 L), sourced from Tronçais (Allier) and Bercé (Sarthe) forests, from 180–350-year-old trees; all barrels replaced each vintage. Barrel room held at constant 14°C. Wine racked manually through the bung hole 5–7 times using nitrogen gas to prevent oxidation. After blending, the assembled Grand Vin returns to barrel for a further ~12 months. Bottling: Fining and filtration practices not publicly specified by the château. ABV varies by vintage (documented examples: 12.8%–14%). Cask strength: no — bottled at standard ABV. Blend/recipe: Vineyard planted ~52% Cabernet Franc, ~43% Merlot, ~5% Cabernet Sauvignon (current plantings; target blend ~55% Cabernet Franc, 40% Merlot, 5% Cabernet Sauvignon). Grand Vin blend varies significantly by vintage: 2023: 46% Cabernet Franc, 52% Merlot, 2% Cabernet Sauvignon; 2021: 52% Cabernet Franc, 33% Merlot, 5% Cabernet Sauvignon; 2020: 30% Cabernet Franc, 65% Merlot, 5% Cabernet Sauvignon; 2019: Cabernet Franc-dominant (exact % not confirmed in sources); 2015: 45% Cabernet Franc, 55% Merlot; 2006: 45% Cabernet Franc, 55% Merlot. Malbec and tiny amounts of Cabernet Sauvignon also present in vineyard. Annual Grand Vin production: ~6,000–7,000 cases. Must know: Cheval Blanc is the only Bordeaux First Growth-equivalent that breaks the Right Bank rule of Merlot dominance — Cabernet Franc ('Bouchet') has been the estate's structural backbone since Jean Laussac-Fourcaud's replanting in the 1860s, a choice considered audacious at the time and still unique today. The 1947 vintage — legendarily scored 100 points by Robert Parker and referenced in Ratatouille and Drops of God — is considered one of the greatest wines ever made; a 6-litre bottle sold at Christie's for $304,375. In 2021, Cheval Blanc and Ausone jointly withdrew from the Saint-Émilion classification after 66 years at its apex, citing criteria that included social-media presence — a decision that, if anything, reinforced rather than diminished their cult status.

parent_region: Pessac-Léognan AOC, Graves, Left Bank Bordeaux (commune of Pessac, ~5 km southwest of central Bordeaux)
description_short_en: Haut-Brion is the oldest continuously operating grand cru in Bordeaux and the only First Growth outside the Médoc — it was classified in 1855 as a special exception, and is the sole château to appear in both the 1855 Médoc Classification…
description_full_en: Founded 1521 (first documented wine production under the name 'Aubrion'); château constructed 1549 by Jean de Pontac. Modern era: purchased 1935 by Clarence Dillon.. Owner: Domaine Clarence Dillon SAS (Dillon family), led by Prince Robert of Luxembourg as Chairman & CEO since 2008. Region: Pessac-Léognan AOC, Graves, Left Bank Bordeaux (commune of Pessac, ~5 km southwest of central Bordeaux). Classification: Premier Grand Cru Classé (1855 Classification) — the only non-Médoc estate in the 1855 list; also classified as Cru Classé de Graves (1953, revised 1959) — the only château ranked in both systems. Style: Haut-Brion produces the most 'urban' and terroir-singular of all First Growths: its Günzian gravel soils and heat-retaining suburban microclimate yield wines of extraordinary savouriness, smoky tobacco, and earthy minerality that are distinctly more approachable in youth than its Médoc peers. The higher Merlot proportion relative to other First Growths imparts a supple, rounded, almost silky texture, while the wine retains exceptional longevity. It is consistently described as the most 'feminine' and perfumed of the five First Growths. Site: 48.35 ha planted to red varieties, situated on two gravel 'croupes' (rises) in Pessac. Soil is rare Günzian gravel (ancient Pleistocene-era quartz pebbles) with some clay parcels — arguably the only true Günzian terroir on the Left Bank. Elevation up to 27 m, above the Bordeaux norm. The surrounding urban buildings create a warmer microclimate, enabling earlier and more reliable ripening; harvest dates are typically among the earliest in Bordeaux. Average vine age ~35 years; oldest parcels date to the 1930s; vine density 8,000 vines/ha. The estate operates one of only three on-site cooperages in Bordeaux. Vinification: Grapes harvested by hand, sorted and de-stemmed; fermented in proprietary double-skinned stainless steel vats (introduced 1991; Haut-Brion was the first Bordeaux estate to use stainless steel in 1961). Maceration ~14–18 days with gentle pump-overs (remontage). Selected yeasts used (Davis 522 preferred for reds). Blending performed after malolactic fermentation, typically by November/December of harvest year — unusually early, before oak influence begins. Aging: 18–24 months in French oak barrels; new oak percentage reviewed annually according to vintage character and wine profile (up to 100% new oak in powerful years). Racking performed traditionally 'à l'esquive' (through the small bunghole), a gentle, precise technique. Estate has its own cooperage. Bottling: Bottled unfiltered. ABV typically ~13–13.5% (varies by vintage). Annual production of grand vin: 10,000–12,000 cases. Blend/recipe: Red grand vin: approximately 45% Merlot, 44% Cabernet Sauvignon, 10% Cabernet Franc, 1% Petit Verdot (planted proportions per Wikipedia/official site; actual blend varies by vintage — e.g. 2021 was reported as ~50% Merlot, 38% Cab Sauv, 12% Cab Franc). Notably higher Merlot share than Médoc First Growths. Must know: Haut-Brion is the oldest continuously operating grand cru in Bordeaux and the only First Growth outside the Médoc — it was classified in 1855 as a special exception, and is the sole château to appear in both the 1855 Médoc Classification and the 1959 Graves Classification. It pioneered virtually every technique now standard in fine Bordeaux: barrel-topping, extended vatting for age-worthy wine, stainless steel fermentation (1961), and private clonal selection research (from 1972 with INRA) — making it arguably the birthplace of modern fine wine.

parent_region: Pauillac AOC, Médoc, Left Bank Bordeaux — situated at the northern extreme of Pauillac, bordering Saint-Estèphe
description_short_en: Lafite legally includes a 4.5-hectare Cabernet Sauvignon plot in AOC Saint-Estèphe (lieu-dit 'Blanquet') in its Pauillac Grand Vin under a dérogation held since 1868 — a unique regulatory anomaly among the First Growths. The estate also…
description_full_en: Founded 1234 (first recorded as estate of Gombaud de Lafite); modern viticulture established c.1670–1680 by Jacques de Ségur; Rothschild era began 1868 when Baron James Mayer de Rothschild purchased the estate at auction for 4.4 million francs. Owner: Domaines Barons de Rothschild (Lafite) — Rothschild family (sixth generation); chaired since 2018 by Saskia de Rothschild, daughter of Baron Éric de Rothschild. Region: Pauillac AOC, Médoc, Left Bank Bordeaux — situated at the northern extreme of Pauillac, bordering Saint-Estèphe. Classification: Premier Cru Classé (1er Grand Cru Classé) — 1855 Bordeaux Classification; listed first among the four original First Growths, earning the informal designation 'first among the firsts'. Style: Lafite is defined by extraordinary finesse and aromatic delicacy rather than the sheer power typical of Pauillac peers — its hallmark is a perfumed, cedar-and-lead-pencil nose with silky, finely pixelated tannins and seamless freshness. Compared to Latour (more structured and muscular) or Mouton Rothschild (more opulent and flamboyant), Lafite consistently prioritises elegance, subtlety, and a long, ethereal finish. Its exceptional aging potential — 30 to 50 years or more in great vintages — is matched by a consistency that yields an average Parker rating of 96.5 across 16 vintages since 1995. Site: 112 hectares divided into three sections: (1) hillside slopes surrounding the château proper, with gentle rises up to 27 metres elevation; (2) the Carruades Plateau to the west; (3) a 4.5-hectare plot in the commune of Saint-Estèphe (lieu-dit 'Blanquet'), included under a historic dérogation granted since 1868. Soil is deep fine gravel (up to 10 metres deep in some parcels) mixed with aeolian black sand over a tertiary limestone subsoil — quaternary alluvial deposits from the Dordogne and Garonne rivers. The Gironde estuary moderates temperatures and protects against frost. Oldest plot, 'La Gravière', planted 1886; some pre-phylloxera ungrafted vines survive. Average vine age ~40 years overall; Grand Vin uses only vines 20+ years old (average ~45 years for Grand Vin parcels). Estate holds ISO 14001, HVE certification (since 2018), has been in organic conversion since 2021, and achieved B Corp status in 2023–2024. Vinification: Grapes harvested by hand and sorted manually in the vineyard plus optically at the winery. Cold pre-fermentation maceration (~5 days) precedes alcoholic fermentation in a combination of 66 temperature-controlled vats — traditional oak, stainless steel, and concrete — selected parcel by parcel according to grape provenance and ripeness. Fermentation at ~28–30°C with regular pump-overs; total maceration ~20–22 days. Malolactic fermentation occurs naturally (no added bacteria) in smaller stainless-steel foudres (25–60 hL). Grand Vin selection finalised in March following harvest. Aging: 18–20 months in 100% new French oak barriques produced at La Tonnellerie des Domaines, Lafite's own cooperage on the Pauillac estate (capacity ~2,000 barrels/year). After the first year, barrels are moved to the grand circular cellar designed by architect Ricardo Bofill (opened 1987). Interventions such as bâtonnage are kept to a minimum. Bottling: Bottled at the château; not chill-filtered (standard Bordeaux practice); fining details not publicly specified. ABV typically ~13–13.5% (vintage-dependent); not cask strength. Sold 100% through the Bordeaux négociant system en primeur; 95% exported. Blend/recipe: Vineyard planted: 70% Cabernet Sauvignon, 25% Merlot, 3% Cabernet Franc, 2% Petit Verdot. Grand Vin blend: typically 80–95% Cabernet Sauvignon, 5–20% Merlot, up to 3% Cabernet Franc and Petit Verdot combined — in practice the Grand Vin is almost always close to 90% Cabernet Sauvignon (e.g. 2016: 92% Cabernet Sauvignon, 8% Merlot; 2024: 96% Cabernet Sauvignon, 3% Merlot, 1% Petit Verdot). Cabernet Franc has become increasingly rare in the Grand Vin in recent vintages. Must know: Lafite legally includes a 4.5-hectare Cabernet Sauvignon plot in AOC Saint-Estèphe (lieu-dit 'Blanquet') in its Pauillac Grand Vin under a dérogation held since 1868 — a unique regulatory anomaly among the First Growths. The estate also operates its own barrel cooperage on-site, giving it rare control over oak character; this, combined with plot-by-plot vinification across ~100 separate parcels, is central to Lafite's house consistency and the ethereal, cedar-inflected style that distinguishes it from all other Pauillac First Growths.

parent_region: Pomerol, Right Bank Bordeaux (commune of Pomerol, Gironde). NOTE: From the 2025 vintage onward, all estate wines are declassified to Vin de France by the Guinaudeau family's own decision.
description_short_en: Lafleur is the only estate in Pomerol to carry genuine pre-1956 Bouchet genetics — a distinct biotype of Cabernet Franc preserved through massale selection by André Robin in the 1930s and saved from the 1956 frost by the Robin sisters;…
description_full_en: Founded 1872 by Henri Greloud. Owner: Guinaudeau family (Société Civile du Château Lafleur) — Jacques & Sylvie Guinaudeau, sole owners since 2002; day-to-day management by Baptiste & Julie Guinaudeau (6th generation) since 2012. Region: Pomerol, Right Bank Bordeaux (commune of Pomerol, Gironde). NOTE: From the 2025 vintage onward, all estate wines are declassified to Vin de France by the Guinaudeau family's own decision.. Classification: Pomerol AOC (unclassified — Pomerol has no official classification system); historically ranked among the appellation's very top growths alongside Pétrus. From 2025 vintage: Vin de France.. Style: Lafleur is defined by an almost equal split of Merlot and Bouchet (the estate's own massale-selected Cabernet Franc clone), making it the highest-Cabernet-Franc Pomerol — a profile far closer to Saint-Émilion than to its Merlot-dominant neighbours. The result is a wine of coiled, floral intensity, iron-edged minerality, and extraordinary longevity, distinct from the plush, clay-driven opulence of Pétrus next door. Production is capped at roughly 1,000 cases per year, and in sub-standard years no grand vin is released at all, enforcing a quality discipline rare even among Bordeaux's elite. Site: 4.58-hectare single-block vineyard on the northern crest of the Pomerol plateau, situated directly adjacent to Château Pétrus and contiguous with Vieux Château Certan and Hosanna. The site is planted in a near-perfect square at approximately the highest elevation point in the appellation. Soils are a mosaic of four distinct micro-terroirs: (1) gravel hillock with brown gravelly clay to the northwest; (2) brown sandy-gravel over gravelly-clay subsoil to the south; (3) brown sandy-gravel over sandy-clay subsoil to the east; (4) a central 0.8-hectare diagonal crescent of gravelly-clayey sand (deeper, richer, with ferric clay subsoil — source of Les Pensées). An iron-rich 'crasse de fer' sublayer runs beneath much of the property. Vine density: ~7,500–8,000 vines/ha on gravel-clay parcels, ~5,250 vines/ha on the central sandy section. Average vine age ~40 years; Bouchet vines trace back to André Robin's 1930s massale selection, preserved through the 1956 frost by cutting damaged vines to ground level rather than uprooting. No chemicals or pesticides have ever been used; ploughing was done by ox until 1979. Farming: sustainable/organic/biodynamic. Vinification: Hand-harvest with parcel-by-parcel picking timed to individual ripeness; double sorting (vineyard + sorting table). Fermentation in small traditional concrete vats (some stainless steel also used depending on cuvée); malolactic fermentation completed in barrel. Maceration approximately 3 weeks, adjusted to vintage character. No enzymes, inoculation, chaptalization, or acidification; all work done by gravity. Aging: 18 months in French oak barriques; approximately 50% new oak, remainder used barrels sourced from sister estate Grand Village. Racked every three months during élevage. Bottling: No fining, no filtration. Natural ABV (varies by vintage; 14.5% noted for 2022). Cask strength not applicable. Blend/recipe: Grand Vin: approximately 50% Merlot / 50% Bouchet (estate massale-selection Cabernet Franc), though the blend shifts vintage to vintage — recent releases have ranged from 46–56% Bouchet. The vineyard is planted across 24 interplanted parcels of both varieties. Les Pensées de Lafleur (own cru since ~2000, from the 0.8-ha central clay parcel): typically Merlot-dominant (~60% Merlot / 40% Bouchet), aged 18 months in 50% new oak. Must know: Lafleur is the only estate in Pomerol to carry genuine pre-1956 Bouchet genetics — a distinct biotype of Cabernet Franc preserved through massale selection by André Robin in the 1930s and saved from the 1956 frost by the Robin sisters; winemaker Omri Ram asserts only Lafleur, Cheval Blanc, and Ausone possess 'real Bouchet,' giving the wine an aromatic and structural fingerprint unmatched anywhere on the Right Bank. In a landmark break with tradition, the Guinaudeau family announced in August 2025 that all six estate wines would be reclassified as Vin de France from the 2025 vintage, citing the need for viticultural flexibility in response to accelerating climate change — a seismic departure for one of Bordeaux's most storied addresses.

description_short_en: In 2012, Château Latour became the first major Bordeaux First Growth to withdraw entirely from the en primeur (futures) system, releasing wines only when deemed ready to drink — typically 8–15 years post-vintage — a radical break from…
description_full_en: Founded Estate documented from 1331 (fortress authorization); wine production lineage formalised under the de Ségur family from c.1670; modern winery identity consolidated by Nicolas-Alexandre de Ségur from 1718. Owner: François Pinault / Groupe Artémis (acquired 1993); day-to-day management by CEO Frédéric Engerer since 1995. Region: Pauillac AOC, Médoc, Left Bank Bordeaux — southeastern tip of the commune, bordering Saint-Julien. Classification: Premier Cru Classé (1855 Bordeaux Classification) — one of only five First Growths. Style: Latour produces the most structurally dense and longest-lived of the five First Growths, built on a dominant Cabernet Sauvignon core from the walled L'Enclos parcel that delivers massive tannic architecture, concentrated cassis, graphite, tobacco and truffle, requiring a minimum of 15 years to open. Unlike Margaux's perfumed elegance or Mouton's opulence, Latour is celebrated for its consistency across difficult vintages — the Gironde's moderating microclimate allows slower, more even ripening that protects quality even in cool years. Site: L'Enclos: a 47-hectare walled parcel surrounding the château, situated ~300 m from the Gironde Estuary, largely unchanged since 1794. Soils are deep gravel croupes over clay-slate subsoil in multiple colours (blue, red, grey); the centre of L'Enclos has the deepest gravel and clay, providing power and depth, while the northern and southern edges carry more clay and marl. The proximity of the Gironde moderates temperature, reduces frost risk, and enables longer, slower ripening. Total estate: 92 hectares (78 ha under vine); L'Enclos is farmed fully biodynamically; entire estate certified organic since 2018–2019. Vines average 40–60 years old; some parcels (e.g. Le Sarmentier) exceed 100 years. Horses have been used for soil work since 2008 to minimise compaction. Vinification: Parcel-by-parcel fermentation in 80 temperature-controlled stainless steel vats ranging from 12 to 164 hL (introduced in the 1960s, renovated 1997 and 2001). Fruit is sorted at both bunch and berry level, fully de-stemmed, then fermented with approximately 3 weeks of skin contact. Malolactic fermentation completed in tank. Gravity-flow cellar since 2001 renovation. Aging: Grand Vin: 100% new French oak barriques, approximately 18–24 months. Les Forts de Latour: 50% new French oak. Blending assemblage begins in January following harvest. Estate holds an underground barrel cellar dug below the water table — one of the only subterranean aging cellars in the Médoc. Bottling: Fined with egg whites; racked before bottling. Not stated as unfiltered or chill-filtered. ABV typically ~13–13.5% (not cask strength). Grand Vin released 8–10 years post-vintage; Les Forts de Latour 6–8 years; Pauillac de Latour 4–6 years — estate withdrew from en primeur in 2012. Blend/recipe: Grand Vin (L'Enclos only): typically ~75–80% Cabernet Sauvignon, ~18–20% Merlot, remainder Cabernet Franc and Petit Verdot. Vineyard-wide plantings: 74.2% Cabernet Sauvignon, 23.8% Merlot, 1.8% Cabernet Franc, 0.02% Petit Verdot. Recent releases can skew higher in Cabernet Sauvignon (e.g. 2017: 92.1% Cabernet Sauvignon, 7.8% Merlot, trace Petit Verdot). Must know: In 2012, Château Latour became the first major Bordeaux First Growth to withdraw entirely from the en primeur (futures) system, releasing wines only when deemed ready to drink — typically 8–15 years post-vintage — a radical break from Bordeaux commercial tradition that no other First Growth has replicated. Latour was also a pioneer in adopting stainless steel fermentation vats in the 1960s, two decades before the practice became standard across Europe.
